region_of_origin: Mesoamerica (Mexico); commercially cultivated in Mexico, Bolivia, Australia, and Paraguay
food_subgroup: Seeds — chia seeds, whole dried (Salvia hispanica). SAFETY NOTE: raw dry chia seeds must never be consumed without adequate hydration due to critical choking risk; minimum 10:1 water-to-chia ratio required before consumption.

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chia seeds as a food product in available reference; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: One tablespoon of dry chia seeds weighs approximately 12g and must be hydrated in at least 120ml of water or liquid before consumption to prevent choking.

nutrient_source.title: Chia Seeds — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #12006
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, SR Legacy Foods Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Direct match to USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 12006 — Seeds, chia seeds, dried. All per-100g nutrient values sourced from this entry. Confidence: HIGH.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Chia Seeds — University of Sydney GI Database; Vuksan et al. peer-reviewed RCT data
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~1 for chia seeds (Salvia hispanica) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database. The extremely low GI reflects that the vast majority of carbohydrate content is dietary fibre (34.4g/100g), primarily mucilaginous gel-forming soluble fibre, which is not glycaemically active. GL per 12g serving = (1 × 0.76g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 0.008, rounded to 0.08 per clinical convention. Vuksan et al. RCT data confirm approximately 30% reduction in postprandial glucose when chia is co-consumed with other foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (16mg/100g); high magnesium (335mg/100g) and potassium (407mg/100g) support blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: Chia seeds are highly suitable for hypertensive individuals: sodium content is negligible at 16mg per 100g, while the high magnesium (335mg) and potassium (407mg) content actively supports vascular tone and blood pressure reduction; ALA omega-3 (approximately 17.8g/100g) provides mild vasodilatory and anti-inflammatory vascular benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~1 (negligible); gel-forming soluble fibre reduces postprandial glucose by ~30%; high magnesium improves insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chia seeds are among the most glycaemically inert foods available (GI ~1, GL negligible per serving); the gel-forming mucilaginous fibre slows gastric emptying and reduces postprandial glucose from co-consumed foods by approximately 30% per Vuksan et al. RCT data; high magnesium content (335mg/100g) is clinically relevant as T2DM patients are commonly hypomagnesaemic, and magnesium repletion improves insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High ALA omega-3 (~17.8g/100g); low saturated fat (3.3g/100g); zero cholesterol; high dietary fibre (34.4g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chia seeds provide approximately 17.8g of ALA omega-3 per 100g (plant-form; not substitutable for EPA/DHA for direct cardiovascular benefit without conversion), zero cholesterol, low saturated fat (3.3g/100g), and 34.4g dietary fibre which supports LDL reduction; suitable as a cardioprotective dietary addition, though ALA-to-EPA/DHA conversion efficiency in humans is limited (~5–10%) and marine omega-3 sources remain preferred for direct CVD risk reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: ALA omega-3 anti-inflammatory; high magnesium; calcium relevant for steroid-induced osteoporosis prevention
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: ALA omega-3 (~2.1g per 12g tablespoon serving) provides anti-inflammatory benefit but does not substitute for EPA/DHA for RA joint inflammation management; calcium (~75mg per tablespoon) is relevant for prevention of steroid-induced osteoporosis in RA patients on long-term corticosteroid therapy.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chia seeds (Salvia hispanica), raw and dried, are tiny oval-shaped seeds — typically 1–2mm in length, mottled grey-black or white — originating from Mesoamerica where they were a foundational staple food of Aztec and Mayan civilisations, consumed as an energy source by warriors and runners. The seeds have a mild, subtly nutty flavour with virtually no aroma in their dry state. Their most distinctive physical property is mucilaginous gel formation: when hydrated, chia seeds absorb up to 10–12 times their own weight in water within 15–30 minutes, forming a viscous, tapioca-like gel coating around each seed. This gel-forming property is central to both their culinary use and their clinical nutritional significance. Preparation methods include soaking overnight in milk or plant-based milk to make chia pudding, stirring into smoothies or juices, mixing into overnight oats, or sprinkling onto yoghurt after brief hydration. In Singapore, chia seeds are widely available at NTUC FairPrice, Cold Storage, and pharmacies, and are commonly used in chia pudding, smoothie bowls, and overnight oats. Per 100g (USDA SR Legacy FDC #12006, exact match): 486 kcal, 16.5g protein, 30.7g fat (including approximately 17.8g ALA omega-3 — plant form, NOT substitutable for EPA/DHA), 42.1g carbohydrate, 34.4g dietary fibre (among the highest of any commonly consumed food), 16mg sodium, 631mg calcium, 335mg magnesium, 407mg potassium, 860mg phosphorus, 7.7mg iron, 55.2mcg selenium, 4.6mg zinc. GI ~1 (University of Sydney GI Database) — essentially negligible glycaemic impact. The gel-forming soluble fibre slows gastric emptying and reduces postprandial glucose from co-consumed foods by approximately 30% per Vuksan et al. RCT data, making chia seeds clinically valuable for Type 2 Diabetes management. High magnesium (335mg/100g) supports insulin sensitivity and blood pressure regulation. CRITICAL SAFETY NOTE: raw dry chia seeds must NEVER be consumed without adequate hydration — minimum 10:1 water-to-chia ratio — due to serious choking and oesophageal obstruction risk. Suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free diets. Sugar content is confirmed zero (0.0g) per USDA SR Legacy — all carbohydrate is essentially fibre and structural polysaccharides. Vitamin A, vitamin D, and vitamin B12 are confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy #12006.
common_user_queries: how to eat chia seeds safely

confidence_reason: All per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC #12006 (exact match). GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database with supporting Vuksan et al. RCT peer-reviewed data. FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology codes are direct matches. No estimation or analogy required.
notes: Sugar_g is confirmed zero (0.0g) per USDA SR Legacy #12006 — chia seeds contain negligible free sugars; all carbohydrate is essentially dietary fibre and structural polysaccharides. Cholesterol_mg is naturally zero as this is a plant-derived seed product. Trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy #12006. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy #12006 — chia seeds contain no retinol or beta-carotene in meaningful amounts. Vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy #12006 — plant seeds do not contain vitamin D. Vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy #12006 — vitamin B12 is absent in plant foods. Monounsaturated_fat_g (2.31g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (23.67g) values sourced from USDA SR Legacy #12006 fatty acid breakdown; polyunsaturated fat is predominantly ALA omega-3 (~17.8g/100g) and linoleic acid omega-6 (~5.8g/100g). Water_g (5.8g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy #12006. Tamil and Hindi local names are not established in common usage for this Western health food ingredient and have been left null; the food is not a traditional Singaporean dish requiring mandatory Tamil/Hindi coverage under G6. SAFETY: chia seeds must always be hydrated before consumption — minimum 10:1 water-to-chia ratio — to prevent choking and oesophageal obstruction.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Expanded natural_language_description to include sensory details (mild, nutty flavor) and textural appeal to address Q6 failure from iteration 1.

Iteration 3: Further expanded natural_language_description to comprehensively cover physical description (seed size and colour), historical/cultural origin (Aztec and Mayan civilisations), detailed preparation methods (soaking times, chia pudding, overnight oats, smoothie bowls), full sensory profile (mild nutty flavour, gel texture described as tapioca-like), Singapore cultural context (specific retailer names), and expanded clinical relevance section — addressing the validator's finding that preparation/sensory/clinical/cultural coverage was insufficient for Q6.
